failure_modes.why: BLUE built only 2 settlements all game and never expanded beyond its initial placements, capping its board presence.
BLUE never upgraded a single settlement to a city, forgoing the easiest VP and production doubling.
BLUE churned countless maritime trades and bought many dev cards but rarely converted resources into permanent builds or VP.

review_summary: BLUE stalled its physical engine—no new settlements, no cities—and frittered resources on trades/dev cards while RED steadily expanded and upgraded to 7 VP.

0.summary: A defensible opening settlement on a port node with zero oracle regret, slightly imperfect on diversity/room but no clear mistake.

29.summary: BLUE makes the optimal robber move, blocking RED and stealing, matching the oracle exactly.

138.summary: RED's robber move matches the oracle's best legal option, blocking BLUE while stealing, a clean play across all criteria.

83.summary: With no buildable resources, buying a dev card is a solid productive use of the hand and superior to passing.

245.summary: Optimal robber placement targeting RED, matching the oracle's best legal move.

317.summary: A defensible bank trade converting surplus wheat into a needed brick, though with only one resource it doesn't unlock any immediate build.

448.summary: Optimal robber placement on a RED tile to steal and block while leading.

508.summary: Optimal robber placement and knight timing, stealing from and blocking BLUE with no regret.

389.summary: RED makes a sound robber placement against the leading BLUE with no regret, satisfying all criteria.

598.summary: A defensible bank trade converting surplus wheat into brick to set up a build while leading, with no downside to the opponent.

645.summary: A sensible 2:1 brick-for-wheat maritime trade matching the oracle's best move with zero regret, advancing toward a city.

failed.claude: Behind in VP, BLUE should have moved robber to a RED-adjacent tile (e.g. (0,1,-1) RED) to steal and slow the leader, not an unstealable tile.
failed.openai: The timing is appropriate as Blue is behind and needs to disrupt Red's progress.

722.summary: BLUE placed the robber on a tile without stealing from the leading RED, wasting an opportunity to both steal a card and block RED's production.

915.summary: A defensible robber placement that blocks RED but passes on a tile that could both block and attempt a steal.

857.summary: RED's robber move to (2,-2,0) targeting BLUE matches the oracle's best play and optimally disrupts the opponent.

783.summary: A sound free-road build that maintains tempo, though a marginally better routing was available.
